skill.name: use-my-browser
skill.description: Browser automation strategy skill that teaches AI agents when to use public-web tools, live Chrome sessions, or separate browser contexts for web research, page inspection, and dynamic site interaction.
skill.summary: Strategic browser automation skill for AI agents - choose the right tool layer for web tasks while preserving user sessions.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: This skill contains documentation-only Markdown files teaching AI agents browser automation strategies. All 219 static analyzer findings are false positives - backticks in code blocks are markdown formatting for command examples, not actual shell execution. URLs are documentation references, not network calls. No executable code, cryptographic functions, or credential access patterns exist in this skill.

content.user_title: Master browser automation with strategic tool selection
content.value_statement: Web automation tasks require different approaches - some need your live browser session, others work better with public-web tools. This skill teaches AI agents to choose the right tool layer for each task while preserving your browser state.
actual_capabilities: Choose between public-web tools, live Chrome sessions, and isolated browser contexts based on task requirements
Continue from user's existing DevTools context (selected elements or network requests)
Extract data from dynamic, authenticated, or anti-bot-protected websites
Preserve user's browser session while performing automation tasks
Handle lazy-loaded content, login flows, and complex web applications
Run parallel research across multiple independent web targets
limitations: Requires Chrome DevTools MCP server setup for live browser session access
Cannot access browser sessions if Chrome is not already running
Not suitable for purely local file operations without web involvement
Respects user preferences - will not touch browser if explicitly requested not to

use_cases.title: Debug failing web requests
use_cases.description: Continue from a network request already selected in DevTools to diagnose 403 errors, failed API calls, or authentication issues without reproducing the problem from scratch.

use_cases.title: Research dynamic websites
use_cases.description: Extract content from social platforms, logged-in dashboards, and JavaScript-heavy sites where static fetches fail to capture the real content.
use_cases.target_user: Researchers and analysts gathering web data

use_cases.title: Automate browser workflows
use_cases.description: Perform actions on authenticated websites like posting content, checking dashboards, or comparing product pages while reusing existing login sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic page inspection
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://example.com and analyze its page structure. Tell me what main sections and interactive elements are present without disrupting my current browser tabs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick page analysis for content discovery

prompt_templates.title: DevTools handoff debugging
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a failing network request selected in Chrome DevTools that returns 403. Use the current DevTools session to analyze the request headers, response, and failure cause.

prompt_templates.title: Authenticated site research
prompt_templates.prompt: Access my logged-in dashboard at https://app.example.com and summarize the key metrics shown. Do not log me out or close any tabs I am using.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-site comparison
prompt_templates.description: Compare pricing pages across multiple competitor websites
prompt_templates.prompt: Research the pricing pages of these 5 SaaS products: [list URLs]. Compare their tiers, features, and pricing models. Use parallel research where possible and cite your sources.

output_examples.input: Check my logged-in analytics dashboard and tell me the traffic trend
output_examples.output: I accessed your analytics dashboard using your current browser session. The dashboard shows a 15% increase in sessions over the past 7 days, with organic search driving most growth. Key metrics: 12,450 sessions (up from 10,820), bounce rate 42% (improved from 48%), average session duration 3:24. Your top landing page is /blog/automation-guide with 2,100 visits.

output_examples.input: Why is this API request failing with 403?
output_examples.output: I analyzed the selected network request in DevTools. The 403 error occurs because the Authorization header is missing. The request goes to /api/v2/data but your session token was not included. The server response says 'Authentication required'. You may need to re-authenticate or check if your token expired.

output_examples.input: Find the real video source URL on this lazy-loaded page
output_examples.output: I inspected the page and found the video is loaded dynamically. The actual video source is: https://cdn.example.com/videos/tutorial-1080p.mp4 (direct download available). The page uses lazy-loading with IntersectionObserver, which is why the video element was not in the initial HTML.

best_practices: Always define the success condition before choosing which browser tool to use - start with the cheapest layer that can succeed
Prefer DOM reads and network inspection over screenshots - structured data is more reliable than pixel analysis
Preserve the user's browser session - do not close tabs you did not open, and use dedicated working tabs for exploration
anti_patterns: Using the live browser session for tasks that only need public citations - start with web search tools instead
Taking screenshots before checking if the DOM or network layer already contains the needed information
Ignoring currently selected DevTools elements or requests and starting analysis from scratch

faq.question: What do I need to set up before using this skill?
faq.answer: You need Chrome (version 144 or later) running with remote debugging enabled, and the Chrome DevTools MCP server configured to auto-connect. The skill works best when Chrome is already open before starting your AI agent session.

faq.question: Will this skill disrupt my current browser tabs?
faq.answer: No, the skill is designed to preserve your session. It reuses your current page only when that is the point of the task, otherwise it opens dedicated working tabs and avoids closing or reloading pages you may still need.

faq.question: Can this skill access my logged-in accounts?
faq.answer: The skill can work with your existing signed-in sessions if you choose to use the live browser option. This is useful for accessing dashboards or authenticated content. You can also opt for isolated browser contexts that do not have access to your sessions.

faq.question: What is the difference between chrome-devtools and playwright options?
faq.answer: Chrome DevTools reuses your current browser session with your cookies, login state, and open tabs. Playwright opens a separate clean browser context without your session data - use it for isolated automation that should not touch your personal browsing state.

faq.question: Can this skill run multiple research tasks in parallel?
faq.answer: Yes, for independent targets the skill can batch public-web searches and structure comparisons across multiple sites efficiently. However, it avoids parallel operations that depend on a single mutable page state to prevent conflicts.

faq.question: What if I do not want the agent touching my browser?
faq.answer: You can explicitly tell the agent not to use your live browser session. The skill will then fall back to public-web tools, processed reads, raw fetches, or isolated browser automation depending on what the task requires.
